channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Move Past the AI Hype: 10 Actual Use-Cases for Large Language Models from Engineers
executive_summary: Engineers are increasingly using large language models (LLMs) to tackle boring or complex coding tasks, which helps them save time and reduce mental effort. Key applications include understanding APIs, diffing code, and writing documentation. By leveraging LLMs, engineers can focus on more critical aspects of their work and improve their overall productivity.
bullets: Engineers are using large language models (LLMs) to perform tasks that are often boring or difficult, which helps them focus on more important work. This is like having a personal assistant who takes care of the tedious chores, allowing you to concentrate on what you truly enjoy.

LLMs assist in understanding APIs, which are like the menus in a restaurant that tell you what dishes you can order. Without a clear menu, you might struggle to know what options are available, but LLMs clarify these choices for engineers.

Diffing code, or comparing different versions of code, is made easier with LLMs. Imagine trying to spot the differences between two similar paintings; LLMs act like a magnifying glass that highlights the subtle changes, making it simpler to identify what has been altered.

Trimming and refactoring code is another task where LLMs shine. Think of it like pruning a tree; just as you cut away unnecessary branches to help the tree grow better, LLMs help streamline code for improved performance.

Writing documentation is often seen as a tedious task, but LLMs can generate clear and concise documentation quickly. It's like having a skilled writer who can take your rough notes and turn them into a polished article in no time.

LLMs are particularly useful for writing throwaway code, which is code that is not meant to last. This is similar to sketching a quick draft before creating a final masterpiece; the draft helps you work through ideas without the pressure of perfection.

Engineers can use LLMs to learn new programming languages, making the learning curve less steep. It's like having a tutor who can explain complex concepts in simple terms, making it easier to grasp new material.

Building apps becomes more manageable with the help of LLMs, which can assist in generating code snippets. Imagine having a toolbox where every tool you need is right at your fingertips, speeding up the construction process.

LLMs help break down technical requirements into manageable parts, which is crucial for project success. This is like dissecting a complicated recipe into simpler steps, making it easier to follow and execute.

The common theme in using LLMs is to lighten the cognitive load for engineers, allowing them to work more efficiently. It's like carrying a heavy backpack; when someone helps you carry it, you can move faster and with less strain.

Nate B Jones emphasizes that it is 'faster to get a clear explanation from an LLM than inferring from Google results.' This highlights the efficiency of LLMs in providing direct answers compared to sifting through multiple search results.

The use of LLMs in coding is changing the time value of coding work, allowing engineers to accomplish more in less time. This shift is like upgrading from a bicycle to a motorcycle; you can cover the same distance much faster.
key_takeaways: Incorporate LLMs into your coding workflow to handle boring or complex tasks. This will free up your time for more critical projects, and without this integration, you risk falling behind in productivity.

Set a goal to use LLMs for at least 50% of your documentation tasks. This will ensure that your documentation is clearer and more efficient, and if you neglect this, your documentation may remain inconsistent and hard to understand.

Aim to use LLMs for learning new programming languages within the next month. This will accelerate your learning curve, and if you delay, you may miss out on valuable skills that could enhance your career.

technical_terms.APIs: Application Programming Interfaces, which allow different software programs to communicate with each other. Think of it like a waiter taking your order at a restaurant and bringing it back from the kitchen.
technical_terms.Diffing code: The process of comparing two versions of code to identify changes. Like checking two drafts of a letter to see what edits were made.
technical_terms.Refactoring: The process of restructuring existing computer code without changing its external behavior. Think of it like renovating a house to make it more efficient without changing its overall look.
technical_terms.Documentation: Written text that explains how a software program works. Like a user manual for a gadget that helps you understand how to use it effectively.
technical_terms.Throwaway code: Code that is written for temporary use and not intended to be part of the final product. It's like a rough draft that you discard after writing the final version.
technical_terms.Cognitive load: The amount of mental effort being used in the working memory. Think of it like carrying a heavy backpack; the more weight you carry, the harder it is to move.
technical_terms.Programming languages: Formal languages used to write computer programs. Like different dialects of a language, each with its own rules and vocabulary.
technical_terms.Code snippets: Small portions of reusable code. Think of them like pre-cut vegetables that save you time when cooking a meal.
technical_terms.Technical requirements: Specifications that define what a software product must do. Like a blueprint that outlines how a building should be constructed.
technical_terms.Learning curve: The rate at which a person learns a new skill. It's like climbing a hill; the steeper the hill, the harder it is to climb at first, but it gets easier with practice.
